headline: LSU's Ground Game Proves Too Much for Miami's High-Flying Offense
body: Miami came to Baton Rouge ready to shoot it out, and for much of the evening Cane quarterback Cody Mertz answered the bell. He carved up the LSU secondary for 322 yards and two touchdowns on 20 completions, and his receivers—particularly the dynamic Dike with 103 yards on just three catches—gave the home defense fits. Yet LSU's suffocating run game and ball security discipline ultimately wore down a Miami team that couldn't stop the bleeding on the ground.

Garrett Nussmeier didn't need to be spectacular. He managed his game, moved the chains, and most importantly didn't beat himself, completing 15 of 26 for 204 yards and two touchdowns with no turnovers. Meanwhile, LSU's running back Ferguson and the offensive line established an identity that Miami's defense had no answer for: 181 rushing yards on 13 carries by Ferguson alone, opening lanes for a bruising approach that controlled tempo and kept Mertz's playmakers off the field.

The final margin of 11 points belies how completely LSU controlled the second half. Miami's two fumbles—costly mistakes when chasing points—swung momentum that never came back. While Nussmeier earned Player of the Game accolades for his poise and execution, it was the Tiger front five that won this game in the trenches, imposing their will when it mattered most.
turningPoint: Miami's second fumble in the fourth quarter, coupled with Ferguson's physical rushing late in the game, shifted this from a track meet into a grind Miami simply couldn't match.

unsungHero.name: Ferguson
unsungHero.why: His 13 carries and punch-in touchdown established the run game that dictated LSU's entire offensive identity, yet Nussmeier's clean pocket time overshadowed the foundation Ferguson laid in the trenches.
